title: The Best News
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-24-23 -AM- The Best News - Luke 2_8-20 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why the Best News Is Hard to Appreciate
subpoints: The Christian story is the most widely known story in history — many are numb to it
65% of Americans believe humanity is basically good; 55% of evangelical churchgoers agree
If humanity is basically good, the birth of a Savior is merely heartwarming — not urgent
But if all have sinned and are under God's condemnation, this news is absolutely crucial

outline.title: First: The Good News Is Historically True
subpoints: The birth of Jesus is history — not a fable or a heartwarming legend
If true, God actually broke into human history in fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy
Things are not okay between you and God apart from Jesus Christ
There is more historical evidence for Jesus's birth, life, death, and resurrection than for George Washington being the first president

outline.title: Second: The Good News Is Based on Christ's Unique Person
subpoints: Jesus is fully man — born in Bethlehem, subject to taxes, a wrinkled red newborn
Jesus is fully God — the angel calls Him "the Lord" (Kurios), the same term used for Yahweh in the Old Testament
Jesus is the Christ/Messiah — the anointed King and Priest who fulfilled all Old Testament prophecy
Jesus is the Savior — implying we are helpless, lost, and under God's just condemnation

outline.title: Third: The Good News Is for All People, Especially the Common Person
subpoints: God chose shepherds — the lowest in social status — to receive the announcement
If the gospel required intelligence or money, many would be excluded
The shepherds may have been watching the lambs destined for the Passover sacrifice
They left their Passover lambs behind to go witness the ultimate Passover Lamb

outline.title: Fourth: The Good News Brings Light, Then Fear, Then Joy
subpoints: The shepherds sat in darkness — a picture of every human heart without the Savior
The glory of the Lord shone around them — illustration of the Spirit illuminating the dark heart
Their first response was terror — just as the glory of God's holiness produces awe and conviction
But God does not leave us in terror — the angel spoke words of comfort: 'Do not be afraid'

outline.title: Fifth: The Good News Requires a Personal Response
subpoints: The shepherds responded in faith — they believed and went to Bethlehem
They responded in proclamation — they told everyone what they had seen and heard
They responded in praise — God filling dark hearts with gratitude
They responded in endurance — going back to their ordinary work, praising God in the routine

summary: In this Christmas Eve sermon on Luke 2:8-20, Pastor Lyro identifies two reasons why people fail to appreciate the best news ever told: the story is overly familiar, and many people (including 55% of evangelical churchgoers, according to the 2022 Ligonier State of Theology survey) believe humanity is basically good and therefore have no felt need for a Savior. The sermon then unfolds five aspects of the good news. First, it is historically true — not a legend or fable, but a real breaking-in of God into human history, confronting every person with objective facts. Second, it centers on Christ's unique person: fully human (able to bear human sin), fully God (His sacrifice having infinite worth before God's throne), Messiah (fulfilling all Old Testament prophecy), and Savior (implying that without Him, all are helplessly lost). Third, the news is for all people, especially the common person — God's choice of shepherds, the lowest in society, demonstrates that the gospel requires no intelligence, money, or status. There is also a second reason for the shepherds: they may have been watching the Passover lambs, and in going to see Jesus, they left their lambs behind to encounter the ultimate Passover Lamb. Fourth, the news brings a progression: darkness, then the blinding light of the gospel, then terror at the holiness of God, then the comfort and joy of grace — just as John Newton wrote: "'Twas grace that taught my heart to fear, and grace my fears relieved." Fifth, the news requires a personal response: faith, proclamation, praise, and endurance in the ordinary routine. The sermon closes with John Newton's dying words: "My memory is nearly gone, but I remember two things: that I am a great sinner and that Christ is a great Savior."
transcript:
## Introduction: Why the Best News Is Hard to Appreciate

A few years ago, a famous news anchor responded to the charge that his profession only covered the negative side of everything. He said: "Yes — try to imagine a newscast that only had good news. It would be something like this: 'All planes took off and landed safely last night. In Florida, the orange crop was hit by another night of average weather. The oranges just hung in there and grew. In Kentucky, Toyota announced that 295,000 Camrys would not be recalled because they're functioning just fine.'" His point was that good news isn't always appreciated unless it's against the backdrop of bad news.

Our text this morning tells us of the best news — the best news ever told in the world — but two factors make it difficult for people to appreciate it. First, the Christian story is perhaps the most widely known story in history. As a result, many people, even Christians, shrug it off as not especially exciting or relevant. Second, many people do not realize the dire condition they are in regarding their standing before God and their eternal destiny.

The 2022 Ligonier State of Theology Survey showed that 65% of all Americans believe that humanity is basically good. But then when you look at what evangelical churchgoers said in the same survey, 55% of evangelical churchgoers also believe that humanity is basically good. If that opinion is true, then the story of the birth of Jesus may warm your heart and make you feel good, but it won't be the best news in the world. However, if the Bible is correct in stating that all people have sinned and, apart from Christ, are under God's condemnation, then the news that Christ is born today is hardly just nice — it is the best news in the world and is absolutely crucial for your destiny.

So I want us to look at five aspects of this good news.

## First: The Good News Is Historically True

So many legends — such as Santa Claus — have become intertwined with the Christmas story that people lump them all together and forget that the birth of Jesus Christ as reported in the Bible is true history. It's not a fable. It's not just something nice for us to read at Christmastime.

Now, somebody might ask: who cares if it's history or not? The story about the Virgin Mary, the Christ child, the angels, the shepherds is a heartwarming tale. What difference does it make if it's real or not? Well, it makes all the difference in the world. If it is just a heartwarming legend, you can choose to believe it or disbelieve it — no problem, no difference in your life. But if the story actually happened as reported by Luke, then the birth of Jesus the Savior confronts every one of you with some objective facts that cannot be shrugged off as personal opinion.

The fact that these events happened as reported means that God exists and that He truly broke into human history in the birth of Jesus Christ, in fulfillment of prophecies in the Old Testament. The fact that God actually sent a Savior implies that people like you and me, without the Savior, are alienated from God and desperately need to be reconciled with Him through forgiveness of their sins. Friends, things are not okay between you and God apart from Jesus Christ. Apart from Jesus Christ, the only thing God is going to do for you is pour His wrath upon you.

There is very little in history that is more well-witnessed than the birth of Jesus Christ. There is more evidence that Jesus Christ was born, lived, died, and rose again than there is that George Washington was the first president of the United States. And yet I don't see anybody rushing to disprove George Washington. So you don't just believe in Jesus because it makes you feel warm and happy inside. You believe because this is true.

## Second: The Good News Is Based on Christ's Unique Person

If you look at verse 11, the angel states plainly that Jesus — born of the Virgin Mary — is the Savior who is Christ the Lord. So consider who this Savior is.

First, He is fully man. He was born in the city of David to the descendants of David who were there to register for their taxes — there is nothing more human than paying taxes. Contrary to the popular Christmas carol, the baby did cry in a manger. There was no halo around His head. What the shepherd saw when they came to the manger was a wrinkled, red newborn human baby. Jesus the Savior assumed full humanity so that He might bear the sins of humanity upon Himself.

But this Savior is also fully God. The angel told the shepherds that this one born in Bethlehem was "the Lord" — *Kurios*. We must interpret this title in light of its use in the Old Testament and in Luke's own context. In chapter 2 of Luke, verse 9, the angel of the Lord stood before them and the glory of the Lord shone around them — the same word is used there. And in verse 23, it refers to the law of the Lord, quoting the Old Testament where *Kurios* clearly means Yahweh. If the word means something different in verse 11 than it does in verses 9 and 23, Luke would have told us.

So the Savior had to be human to bear the sin of humans. But He also had to be God so that His sacrifice had merit before the holy throne of Almighty God. The title Lord also implies that Jesus has authority over every person, every angelic being, and every demonic power. It is absurd to say, "I've accepted Jesus as my Savior but not as my Lord." The two go together — Jesus is both Savior and Lord.

The angel also says He was Christ, or Messiah — the Hebrew and Greek terms for "anointed one." This refers to Jesus as the special anointed King and Priest who brings gospel salvation to His people, and it focuses on the fact that Jesus fulfilled all the Old Testament prophecies about the promised Savior.

And finally, He is the Savior — which implies that those He came to save are lost, alienated from God, under His just condemnation. We need outside intervention if we are to be delivered from God's judgment. And Jesus alone provides salvation for sinners.

## Third: The Good News Is for All People, Especially the Common Person

Have you ever considered why the story doesn't say, "Now there were in the same region scribes and Pharisees keeping watch over their scrolls"? Or, "There were kings and princes keeping watch over their treasures at their palace"? God chose to reveal the birth of the Savior to simple shepherds who were going about their duties. Why?

One reason is obvious: God chose shepherds to show that the good news is for all people, not just the elite. The shepherds were the lowest in status in society. If the gospel required years of graduate study or a high IQ, those who attained it would boast of their intelligence. If the gospel required money or high social standing, there would be no hope for the poor and lowly. But the beauty of the gospel is that even an uneducated, illiterate tribal man in a jungle can understand that he is a sinner and that Jesus is God's Savior, and by God's grace he can believe and be saved.

But I think there is a second reason. The good news involved the sacrifice of the Lamb of God. These shepherds watching the sheep by night — those sheep were likely the sheep to be sacrificed in the following Passover. So here we have the shepherds watching over the Passover lambs, leaving their lambs behind to go witness the ultimate Passover Lamb — the Lamb who is going to take away the sin of the world. In His perfect justice, God declared that the wages of sin is death. But in His love and mercy, God provided the very penalty His justice demanded. The entire sacrifice system in Judaism pointed ahead to Jesus Christ, the perfect sin-bearer. Brothers and sisters, if you trust in Him as your sin-bearer, God has counted your guilt as Jesus's guilt, and His righteousness as your righteousness.

## Fourth: The Good News Brings Light, Then Fear, Then Joy

The events that happened to those shepherds on that historic night were symbolic of what happens to every person who responds to the good news of Christ the Savior.

First, they were sitting in the darkness of the Judean night — a picture of every human heart without the Savior. We are all sitting in darkness and the shadow of death. Then, suddenly — a great flash of light. An angel of the Lord stood before them and the glory of the Lord shone around them. This is an illustration of what happens to every person when the Holy Spirit illuminates a dark heart with the light of the gospel. Whereas they were blind, now they see — they see the darkness of their sin and the glory of the Savior.

And we might want to skip straight to joy, but there's one step before that: they were terrified. It's much the same when the light of the gospel flashes upon your mind. Sitting in the darkness of sin may be a bit spooky, but it was tolerable — it was what you knew, what you were comfortable with. But suddenly the glory of God's absolute holiness shines into your sin-darkened heart, and you realize with Isaiah: "Woe is me! For I am ruined, because I am a man of unclean lips, and my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts."

But thankfully, God in His tender mercy does not leave us in that terrified state. The angel immediately spoke words of comfort and joy: "Do not be afraid, for behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy." As John Newton wrote: "'Twas grace that taught my heart to fear, and grace my fears relieved. How precious did that grace appear the hour I first believed."

## Fifth: The Good News Requires a Personal Response

The shepherds did not hear this great news and then sit around discussing it. They didn't send a delegation to the rabbis in Jerusalem to get their view. They responded in faith. They obviously believed the words of the angel — otherwise they would not have left their sheep and gone to Bethlehem.

And what did they see when they got to Bethlehem? They had just witnessed the greatest angel concert in history — lights and flash, angels singing. Then they get to Bethlehem, and what do they see? A newborn baby. They didn't see a kingly child arrayed in royal robes, a golden parade, a ceremonial entourage. They saw a common couple from Nazareth in a primitive stable with a normal-looking newborn baby. It wasn't what you would expect God to bring His anointed Savior into the world. But the shepherds viewed this baby with eyes of faith, according to the word of God given through the angel. When God reveals Christ to your soul, you must respond with eyes of faith.

They also responded with proclamation. The shepherds didn't stop to think about how people might respond. They went and proclaimed what they knew about Christ. Some might have raised their eyebrows and said, "So you saw a bunch of angels and then went and saw a carpenter's wife with a baby in a feeding trough? You might lay off the eggnog." But that didn't stop these men from relating the story to whomever they saw. Brothers and sisters, once you have seen the Savior with eyes of faith, you cannot stop telling others the great news.

They also responded with praise. When God has taken you from the darkness of your sin and by His grace revealed His Savior to your soul, your heart will be filled with praise and joy.

And one more thing: the shepherds also responded with endurance. Verse 20 says that the shepherds went back. Where did they go back to? Not to sign a contract to appear on Christian TV shows. Not to launch a ministry called "Shepherd's Vision" and become famous. They went back to their sheep. Back to regular life. After the great things they saw, they went back to the routine job they had before. They didn't set up tours to Bethlehem or put on seminars on how to have visions of angels. They went back to their jobs — but they did that praising God for His abundant grace to them.

Brothers and sisters, God doesn't call us to spectacular, flashy, constant, exciting lives. He calls us to believe in the Savior, and then He sends us back into the routine — to learn, to rejoice in Him and in His great salvation, day in and day out. It is in the little moments of everyday life that we meet Jesus. The shepherds show us that.

John Newton, when he was 82 years old and nearing his death, suffering from dementia — right before he lost his ability to speak — said these words: "My memory is nearly gone, but I remember two things: that I am a great sinner, and that Christ is a great Savior." If you know those two things personally, you know the best news in the whole world.

Father, if I didn't, I thank You for Your word. We pray that Your word would find a home in our hearts, and we might hold on to Christ. For those who believe, we pray that we will grow in Him in every moment of life, and for those among us who don't believe, we pray that You bring them to salvation through the Lord Jesus Christ. In His name we pray. Amen.
